skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Routes text-to-image and image-to-image requests through the RunComfy CLI. It helps choose documented RunComfy image models, prepare prompts, and run model endpoints with suitable options.
skill.summary: Generate and edit images with RunComfy model routing and prompt guidance.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown backticks, model identifiers, example URLs, and documentation text. The real risks are legitimate but material: the skill permits RunComfy CLI execution, sends prompts and reference URLs to RunComfy services, and writes downloaded outputs to a local directory.

medium_findings.title: External RunComfy CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(runcomfy *) and provides many runcomfy run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it executes an external CLI that can submit jobs, poll remote services, and download generated assets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry and repeated runcomfy run examples confirm external command execution. The command scope is narrow, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Prompt and Reference Data Sent to Third-Party Service
medium_findings.description: The skill routes prompts and image references to RunComfy model endpoints. User prompts, reference image URLs, and web-grounded generation requests may expose sensitive creative or personal data to a third-party provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states that the CLI posts to the RunComfy Model API and accepts image URLs. This confirms third-party data transfer, although the endpoint is directly related to the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Reference Inputs Can Influence Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly treats reference image URLs and enable_web_search results as untrusted because embedded text or web-grounded content can steer model output. This is an indirect prompt injection risk for generated image tasks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill itself documents this risk and gives mitigations. I did not find a malicious prompt injection command in the file, but the data path exists by design.

medium_findings.title: Local Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: The runcomfy examples write generated files to --output-dir ./out, and the skill says the CLI downloads output URLs into that directory. This is normal behavior, but users should understand the local filesystem write side effect.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The output directory flag is repeated in executable examples and the behavior is described in the implementation notes. There is no evidence of arbitrary path traversal in the skill text.

low_findings.title: Static Shell-Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby backtick and shell substitution patterns. The cited content is markdown prose, model identifiers, fenced command examples, or an explicit note that prompt content is not shell-expanded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows markdown inline code and documentation rather than Ruby code. The only real command surface is the declared runcomfy CLI usage captured in the medium findings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Service Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to RunComfy model pages, documentation, related skills, and placeholder HTTPS image examples. These URLs are expected for a RunComfy catalog skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs consistently reference RunComfy, Skills pages, or examples. I did not find evidence of unrelated exfiltration endpoints or hidden callbacks.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Placeholder URLs
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings at the image example lines come from placeholder strings such as https://.../portrait.jpg. They are not filesystem paths and do not instruct local traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequence appears only inside illustrative HTTPS placeholders. No local file path traversal or file read behavior is present at those locations.

low_findings.title: Token Storage Is Disclosed, Not Secret Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hidden-file finding is a disclosure that runcomfy login stores a token under ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600. The skill warns not to echo, log, or commit the token.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line documents normal CLI credential storage and includes handling guidance. I did not find instructions to read or transmit the token outside RunComfy authentication.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm hits appear to be triggered by ordinary markdown and image-model text, not cryptographic operations. No hashing, encryption, signing, or password storage code is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown documentation and command examples. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak crypto usage in executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Scope for runcomfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash(runcomfy *), which lets the assistant run any runcomfy subcommand available in the local CLI installation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit. The scope is limited to runcomfy, but it remains an external command permission.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Model Invocation with Local Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The implementation description says the CLI posts requests to the RunComfy Model API, polls status, and downloads .runcomfy.net or .runcomfy.com outputs into --output-dir.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is stated directly in the file. It is expected for this skill but important for marketplace users to understand.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional Web Grounding
dangerous_patterns.description: The enable_web_search option can add web-grounded information to generations. The skill says to keep it false unless the user explicitly asks for real-world grounding.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The option is documented in the schema and mitigation text. This is not malicious, but it expands the information sources influencing output.

content.value_statement: Image generation workflows need the right model, prompt format, and endpoint options. This skill routes requests to RunComfy models and provides practical commands for generation and edits.
actual_capabilities: Selects RunComfy text-to-image routes for FLUX, GPT Image, Nano Banana, Seedream, Qwen, Wan, and other models.
Provides documented runcomfy CLI invocations for text-to-image generation.
Supports image-to-image edit routes using reference image URLs.
Gives prompt guidance for typography, photoreal portraits, moodboards, multilingual creative assets, and model-specific workflows.
Explains RunComfy CLI setup, authentication options, exit codes, and output download behavior.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Sends prompts, image URLs, and generation requests to RunComfy services.
Does not provide mask-driven inpainting; it points users to a separate image-edit skill.
Model availability, pricing, and schemas may change on RunComfy model pages.

use_cases.title: Create Campaign Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate posters, product images, and social images with model choices for text accuracy or photoreal quality.

use_cases.title: Explore Fast Concepts
use_cases.description: Produce batches of quick visual directions for storyboards, moodboards, and early creative review.

use_cases.title: Edit Reference Images
use_cases.description: Use image-to-image routes to preserve identity, adjust layouts, replace backgrounds, or rewrite visible text.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Image Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a ceramic coffee mug on a marble counter. Use warm morning light and a square format.

prompt_templates.title: Typography Poster
prompt_templates.scenario: Create an image with exact visible text.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a landscape product poster. The headline must read exactly "AURORA Spring 2026". Use clean white sans-serif type on a dark background.

prompt_templates.title: Photoreal Product Shot
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose a model for realistic commercial imagery.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a photoreal product shot of a glass skincare bottle on wet stone. Use soft daylight, natural reflections, and premium editorial styling.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reference Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Edit an existing asset while preserving important details.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the provided reference image. Keep the subject identity and clothing unchanged. Replace the background with a rainy neon street and preserve the camera angle.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a poster with an exact multilingual headline.
output_examples.output: The skill selects GPT Image 2, explains why it fits typography, and returns a RunComfy command structure with the requested text and size choice.

output_examples.input: A user asks for ten fast moodboard concepts.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a fast FLUX or Nano Banana route, suggests draft resolution, and explains how to vary seeds for multiple directions.

output_examples.input: A user asks to restyle a portrait while preserving identity.
output_examples.output: The skill chooses an image-to-image edit route, asks for or uses the supplied reference URL, and emphasizes preservation instructions before the requested change.

best_practices: Use exact quoted text when the image must contain readable words.
Keep enable_web_search disabled unless real-world grounding is required.
Use low resolution for drafts and higher resolution only for final candidates.
anti_patterns: Do not send private or regulated images unless the user approves third-party processing.
Do not enable broad web grounding for fictional or private creative briefs.
Do not use arbitrary output paths when a project-local output directory is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images directly?
faq.answer: It uses the RunComfy CLI to call RunComfy image models and download generated outputs.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it support editing existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents image-to-image edit routes that use one or more reference image URLs.

faq.question: Can it create images with exact text?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends GPT Image 2 for posters, ads, multilingual text, and layout-sensitive image text.

faq.question: What data leaves the local environment?
faq.answer: Prompts, model inputs, reference URLs, and generation requests are sent to RunComfy services.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a marketplace warning. The risks are expected for a CLI-based network image generation skill.
